din is a lead travel writer at nerdwallet. he previously was a technology reporter at politico, where he authored a daily newsletter covering tech and telecom policy. benjamin loves to travel — both for work and for fun. he’s reported from three continents and visited more than 45 countries. he has written for the wall street journal, bloomberg news, the washington post, the san francisco chronicle and the (johannesburg) star, as well as covered two olympics with nbc sports. his goal is to visit a new country and a new state each year. edited by claire tsosie managing editor 12 years of experience claire tsosie is a managing editor for the travel rewards team at nerdwallet. she started her career on the credit cards team as a writer, then worked as an editor on new markets. her work has been featured by forbes, usa today and the associated press. other fact checked how is this page expert verified? nerdwallet's content is fact-checked for accuracy, timeliness and relevance. it undergoes a thorough review process involving writers and editors to ensure the information is as clear and complete as possible. more on our editorial rigor some card info may be outdated this page includes information about these cards, currently unavailable on nerdwallet. the information has been collected by nerdwallet and has not chase ultimate rewards® is one of the most valuable points currencies, especially if you know how to take advantage of the program's transfer partners. if you hold an eligible chase card, you can transfer points at a 1:1 ratio to a roster of 14 airline and hotel partners, including high-value loyalty programs like world of hyatt, which nerdwallet values at 1.8 cents per point. nerdwallet rating read review rates & fees although several chase credit cards earn chase ultimate rewards® points, only three allow you to transfer your points to airline and hotel loyalty programs: chase sapphire preferred® card . chase sapphire reserve® . ink business preferred® credit card . » jump to learn more about these card options . strategically converting ultimate rewards® to hotel or airline programs can help you take advantage of good award travel deals and squeeze more value out of each point. email address by signing up, you will receive newsletters and promotional content and agree to our terms of use and acknowledge the data practices in our privacy policy . you may unsubscribe at any time. » learn more: the best travel credit cards right now the full list of chase transfer partners chase has 14 transfer partners, comprising 10 airline and four hotel loyalty programs. you can transfer chase points at a 1:1 ratio for all partners. airline transfer partners aer lingus aerclub air canada aeroplan air france-klm flying blue british airways club club iberia plus jetblue trueblue singapore airlines krisflyer southwest rapid rewards united mileageplus virgin atlantic flying club hotel transfer partners ihg one rewards marriott bonvoy world of hyatt wyndham rewards which chase transfer partners are the best? there are a lot of chase transfer partners to choose from, but some offer more value than others. that’s because each loyalty program sets its own redemption rates, determining how many points or miles you need to book an award flight or hotel stay. at a glance, here are some top chase transfer partners, based on nerdwallet’s analysis: world of hyatt. jetblue trueblue. singapore airlines krisflyer. virgin atlantic flying club. for a complete list, check out nerdwallet’s guide to the best chase transfer partners . best airline partner for chase points choosing the best airline transfer partner for chase is difficult, because it will largely depend on your travel habits. for example, do you mainly travel within the u.s. or do you frequently go abroad? when you travel, do you prefer flying in first or business class, or are you ok with sitting in economy? your answers to those questions will help determine which chase airline partner is best for you. most people won’t go wrong with transferring their chase points to jetblue trueblue , which can offer a lot of value for both domestic and international flights. you can use those points to redeem flights with jetblue airways, as well as its partners, including condor, etihad airways and qatar airways. plus, chase is the best jetblue transfer partner among transferable rewards currencies, with points transferring at a 1:1 ratio. while other transferable currencies transfer to jetblue, it’s not always 1:1. best chase hotel transfer partner unlike chase’s airline transfer partners, there’s a clear winner when it comes to hotel transfer partners: world of hyatt. it’s also the best chase transfer partner overall, given its eye-popping value of 1.8 cents per point, according to nerdwallet research. world of hyatt is an incredibly valuable transfer partner because it’s one of the few loyalty programs that still uses an award chart , making it easy for you to know how many points you’ll need for an award redemption. transferring your chase points to hyatt can be a great idea, especially because of how difficult it is to earn hyatt points. while some of chase’s transfer partners also partner with other transferable currencies, only chase and bilt rewards allow you to transfer your points to world of hyatt. if you’re a frequent hyatt guest, make sure to transfer your chase points wisely. » learn more: the complete guide to the world of hyatt loyalty program ★ limited time offer heads up! for a limited time, new cardholders of the chase sapphire reserve® can earn a bonus of 150,000 points by meeting the card's initial spending requirement. that's the biggest pile of bonus points the card has ever offered in its nearly 10 years on the market. the bonus is worth $1,500 or more when redeemed for travel. chase spells out the bonus offer like this: earn 150,000 bonus points after you spend $6,000 on purchases in the first 3 months from account opening. learn more and apply here. chase airline transfer partners: what you need to know of the 14 chase ultimate rewards® transfer partners, 11 are airline mileage programs. while not officially part of the oneworld alliance, aer lingus has close ties with several oneworld airlines, including american airlines and british airways. in fact, aer lingus aerclub uses avios, a loyalty currency shared with oneworld members british airways, iberia and qatar airways. you can transfer avios between the four airlines to take advantage of each program’s sweet spots. air canada is a star alliance member, so you can use aeroplan points to book award flights on any star alliance airline, including united airlines. it also partners with carriers like cathay pacific, emirates and etihad airways, so it’s possible to snag an award flight on those airlines as well. nerdwallet estimates the value of aeroplan points at 1.1 cents per point. » learn more: a guide to air canada’s aeroplan rewards loyalty program flying blue is the loyalty program of air france and klm. both airlines are members of the skyteam alliance , so you can transfer points to flying blue and book award flights on other skyteam airlines, such as delta air lines. nerdwallet values flying blue points at 0.8 cent per mile. however, you can easily get more value by taking advantage of perks like free stopovers in amsterdam and paris on award tickets, as well as monthly promo rewards deals for discounted awards to select destinations. » learn more: a points collector’s guide to air france-klm flying blue most travelers think of flights to london when thinking about british airways. however, that's not where the value of british airways avios lies. in fact, most british airways avios sweet spots don't involve flying british airways. instead, use this chase transfer partner to book inexpensive awards on american airlines, alaska airlines and other oneworld partners. if you can’t find an award that suits you, remember you can always transfer your points from british airways club to a different loyalty program that also uses avios. » learn more: the complete guide to the british airways avios program just like british airways, iberia is a member of the oneworld alliance and uses the avios program. iberia sweet spots include some incredible gems, and the spanish airline often offers some of the cheapest business class awards to europe . » learn more: what to know about the club iberia plus loyalty program jetblue trueblue is a revenue-based points program, so the required number of points for an award roughly equates to the cash price of the flight. however, it also offers solid partnerships with other airlines that let you squeeze more value out of your trueblue points. according to nerdwallet’s valuations, trueblue points are worth 1.4 cents per point when redeemed for jetblue economy awards, making it a valuable points currency to have. » learn more: the complete guide to the jetblue trueblue rewards program singapore airlines is another chase travel partner in the star alliance, and it can provide excellent premium cabin redemptions. singapore krisflyer sweet spots include flights to europe and hawaii, and flights within the continental u.s. luxury travelers also flock to krisflyer, as it's typically the only way to book the award-winning singapore first class product with points. » learn more: the guide to singapore airlines’ krisflyer frequent flyer program southwest airlines rapid rewards southwest rapid rewards are worth 1.3 cents per point when redeemed for southwest flights. steer clear of other rapid rewards redemptions for hotels, rental cars or merchandise though, as the redemption value can drop to 0.6 cents per point. » learn more: the complete guide to southwest rapid rewards united is the third of chase’s u.s.-based airline partners. mileageplus miles are worth around 1.2 cents per mile when redeemed for united economy flights . in addition to redeeming miles on united, you can redeem united mileageplus miles for award flights on other star alliance partners. keep in mind that united can charge more miles for partner awards, although it does offer generous award change and cancelation policies. » learn more: the complete guide to united mileageplus program london-based virgin atlantic is a member of the skyteam alliance and it uses a different redemption chart for each partner, meaning the flying club sweet spots are just as diverse as its partners. some popular ones include premium cabin travel on all nippon airways between the u.s. and japan, but availability can be difficult to find. » learn more: the guide to virgin atlantic flying club chase hotel transfer partners: what you need to know chase has four hotel loyalty program partners. the ihg loyalty program can offer good value, but it's generally a weak chase transfer partner. nerdwallet values ihg points at 0.6 cent each. but since chase points transfer to ihg one rewards at the same 1:1 transfer ratio as all other partners, that makes for a pretty bad transfer that can actually devalue your points. unless you find a fantastic ihg redemption, only transfer points from chase to ihg to top off your account if you're a few points shy of booking a hotel you want with your existing stash of ihg points. otherwise, you’re generally better off booking ihg hotels via chase's portal. marriott bonvoy is one of the largest hotel loyalty programs around. you can redeem bonvoy points for free nights and room upgrades at over 7,000 properties across more than 30 brands. however, bigger isn't always better. when nerdwallet analyzed the value of marriott bonvoy points , we found that points were worth around 0.8 cent each. considering chase points can be redeemed for at least 1 cent each in chase's travel portal (depending on which sapphire card you have), make sure that you're getting at least this much value before transferring chase points to marriott bonvoy — which is unlikely. with that, save bonvoy transfers only for situations where you find an outstanding hotel deal, or if you need just a few more points to book using your existing bonvoy points. » learn more: the complete guide to the marriott bonvoy program world of hyatt offers incredible value, largely due to the fact that it still publishes an award chart. that means predictability in planning how many points an award stay may cost. award nights start at 3,500 points, meaning you can really stretch out your chase points at hyatt hotels. however, a new award chart set to take effect may 2026 means many redemptions during peak periods will get more expensive. wyndham rewards also offers a fixed award chart, with only three tiers: 7,500, 15,000 or 30,000 points per room. however, wyndham is known to run points sales, allowing you to purchase them for less than 1 cent apiece. if you're eyeing a wyndham redemption, that may be a better option than transferring points. chase points calculator while you might book hotels and airfares with cash or through chase’s travel portal , it can be a better deal to transfer your chase points to an airline or hotel partner. however, that’s not always the case. before booking, make sure to calculate the value of your chase ultimate rewards® points relative to the price of paying for airfare or hotel rooms in cash. nerdwallet’s chase points calculator can help you determine the value of your points. how to earn chase ultimate rewards® points the most common way to earn chase ultimate rewards® points is through spending on a chase credit card that participates in chase ultimate rewards®. you can also earn a large amount of points by taking advantage of introductory offers, which get you a windfall of points when you meet a minimum spending requirement in a certain time frame. just note that not all chase credit cards earn transferable ultimate rewards® points. cards that earn ultimate rewards® points include: chase sapphire preferred® card chase sapphire reserve® chase freedom flex® chase freedom unlimited® ink business preferred® credit card ink business cash® credit card ink business unlimited® credit card some of these cards offer bonus points in certain categories, so someone seeking to maximize their points earning might have multiple cards in their wallet to use depending on the transaction. to maximize points, you might use the chase sapphire preferred® card to pay at restaurants (it earns 3 points per $1 spent on dining), the chase freedom flex® to pay at drugstores (it earns 3% back at drugstores) and the ink business cash® credit card to pay the monthly internet bill (it earns 5% back on internet, cable and phone services, up to a combined $25,000 in annual spending). even though the latter two cards don't let you transfer your rewards directly to any of the above airline or hotel partners, you can transfer them to another card that you hold (or that someone in your household holds), as long as that card is one of the cards that allows for transfers to partner airline and hotel brands. » read: why the amex gold card deserves a spot in my wallet chase travel cards with transfer partners the four chase travel cards with transfer partners are the chase sapphire preferred® card , the chase sapphire reserve® , the sapphire reserve for business℠ and the ink business preferred® credit card . chase cards with transferable points on chase's website sapphire reserve for business℠ annual fee $95 . $795 . welcome offer earn 75,000 bonus points after you spend $5,000 on purchases in the first 3 months from account opening. earn 150,000 bonus points after you spend $6,000 on purchases in the first 3 months from account opening. earn 150,000 bonus points after you spend $20,000 on purchases in your first 3 months from account opening. earn 100,000 bonus points after you spend $8,000 on purchases in the first 3 months from account opening. rewards • 5x points on all chase travel℠ purchases (exclusions apply). • 5x on lyft rides and peloton equipment (for a limited time; terms apply). • 2x points on all other travel. • 3x points on dining, takeout and eligible delivery worldwide. • 3x points for online grocery purchases (exclusions apply). • 3x points on some streaming services. • 1x point on all other purchases. • 8 points per $1 spent on all travel booked through chase. • 4 points per $1 spent on bookings directly through an airline or hotel. • 3 points per $1 spent on dining, takeout and eligible delivery worldwide. • 1 point per $1 spent on all other purchases. 8 points per $1 on all purchases booked through chase, including the edit. 5 points per $1 on lyft rides through 9/30/27. 4 points per $1 spent on flights and hotels booked direct. 3 points per $1 on social media and search engine advertising. 1 point per $1 on all other purchases. • 3 points per $1 on the first $150,000 spent on travel, shipping, select advertising, internet, cable and phone services business categories each account anniversary year. • 5 points per $1 spent on lyft rides through sept. 30, 2027. how do you transfer chase points? use this guide to learn how to transfer chase points between two credit cards you own. here's how to transfer chase points to a family member or spouse . once you've got all the points you need in one account, you're ready to then send them to the actual airline or hotel loyalty program you want to convert your chase points into. learn how to transfer chase ultimate rewards® points to travel partners here. how to maximize your rewards you want a travel credit card that prioritizes what’s important to you. here are some of the best travel credit cards of 2026 : flexibility, point transfers and a large bonus: chase sapphire preferred® card no annual fee: wells fargo autograph® card flat-rate travel rewards: capital one venture rewards credit card bonus travel rewards and high-end perks: chase sapphire reserve® luxury perks: american express platinum card® business travelers: ink business preferred® credit card Citi ThankYou Transfer Partners: A Complete Guide
With a long list of hotel and airline transfer partners, there's no shortage of ways to use your ThankYou points.
Written by
Benjamin Din
Lead Travel Writer & Content Strategist
11 years of experience
Expertise
loyalty programs
Benjamin Din is a lead travel writer at NerdWallet. He previously was a technology reporter at Politico, where he authored a daily newsletter covering tech and telecom policy.
Benjamin loves to travel — both for work and for fun. He’s reported from three continents and visited more than 45 countries. He has written for The Wall Street Journal, Bloomberg News, The Washington Post, The San Francisco Chronicle and The (Johannesburg) Star, as well as covered two Olympics with NBC Sports.
His goal is to visit a new country and a new state each year.

12 years of experience
Claire Tsosie is a managing editor for the Travel Rewards team at NerdWallet. She started her career on the credit cards team as a writer, then worked as an editor on New Markets. Her work has been featured by Forbes, USA Today and The Associated Press.

How is this page expert verified?

relevance. It undergoes a thorough review process involving

More on our editorial rigor
SOME CARD INFO MAY BE OUTDATED
This page includes information about these cards, currently unavailable on
NerdWallet. The information has been collected by NerdWallet and has not
been provided or reviewed by the card issuer.
Citi ThankYou points are among the most valuable points currencies out there. That’s because you can transfer these points to one of Citi’s airline or hotel loyalty partners. NerdWallet estimates the value of Citi ThankYou points at 1.6 cents each.
But the Citi ThankYou program is probably also the most complicated transferable currency compared to its peers, because not all programs transfer at a 1:1 ratio and the ratio you get depends on which Citi card you hold.
Here’s everything you need to know about Citi’s transfer partners.

How to earn Citi ThankYou points
The full list of Citi ThankYou transfer partners
The majority of Citi’s transfer partners are airlines, with a handful of hotel programs included. You’ll need to have an eligible Citi card to transfer your points to partners, and certain cards offer better transfer ratios than others.
Cards with annual fees offer more favorable transfer ratios. Examples include:
The Citi Strata Elite℠ Card .
The Citi Strata Premier® Card .
Cards with no annual fee tend to offer ratios that are worse in value by 30%. These include:
The Citi Strata℠ Card .
The Citi Double Cash® Card .
The Citi Custom Cash® Card .
Here are Citi’s transfer partners and their corresponding ratios:
American Airlines AAdvantage — 1:1 or 1:0.7.
Avianca LifeMiles — 1:1 or 1:0.7.
Cathay Pacific Asia Miles — 1:1 or 1:0.7.
Emirates Skywards — 1:0.8 or 1:0.56.
Etihad Guest — 1:1 or 1:0.7.
EVA Air Infinity MileageLands — 1:1 or 1:0.7.
Flying Blue — 1:1 or 1:0.7.
JetBlue TrueBlue — 1:1 or 1:0.7.
Qantas Frequent Flyer — 1:1 or 1:0.7.
Qatar Airways Privilege Club — 1:1 or 1:0.7.
Singapore Airlines KrisFlyer — 1:1 or 1:0.7.
Thai Airways Royal Orchid Plus — 1:1 or 1:0.7.
Turkish Airlines Miles&Smiles — 1:1 or 1:0.7.
Virgin Atlantic Flying Club — 1:1 or 1:0.7.
ALL - Accor Live Limitless — 2:1 or 2:0.7.
Choice Privileges — 1:1.5 or 1:1.05.
Leaders Club — 5:1 or 5:0.7.
Preferred Hotels & Resorts — 1:2 or 1:1.4.
Wyndham Rewards — 1:1 or 1:0.7.
Citi also partners with Virgin Red, a sister program to Virgin Atlantic Flying Club, and Shop Your Way, a retail-based program. However, NerdWallet recommends transferring your points to an airline or hotel travel partner to get the most value.

Which Citi transfer partners are the best?
Citi has one of the most robust rosters of transfer partners, including some unique ones like American’s AAdvantage program and Thai Airways’ Royal Orchid Plus. It also transfers 1:1 to EVA Air Infinity MileageLands, which no other major transferable program offers.
Here’s a list of top Citi transfer partners that have broad general appeal, according to NerdWallet’s analysis:
Singapore KrisFlyer
For a complete guide, check out NerdWallet’s guide to the best (and worst) Citi transfer partners .
There’s no question that Citi’s best airline transfer partner is American Airlines. Its AAdvantage program offers excellent value for points, whether you’re looking to book domestic flights on American or international flights on the carrier or one of its Oneworld alliance partners. If you’re looking for a last-minute flight, you can almost always count on American to have availability at reasonable award prices. (NerdWallet values American miles at 1.3 cents each when booking economy flights.)
American miles are also an excellent choice for redeeming bucket-list flights, such as Japan Airlines first class or Finnair’s innovative “no recline” business class seat. Those premium redemptions are pricey, so they have largely been out of reach for many until July 2025, when Citi added American as a transfer partner — the only major transferable currency that currently does so.
Hotel transfer partners are usually lackluster, because their points aren’t always worth that much. There are a few exceptions to that rule, and Choice Privileges is one of them. Citi is not the only major transferable program that transfers to Choice, but it does offer a higher 1:1.5 transfer ratio — meaning you get 1.5 Choice points for every Citi point you transfer. (However, Wells Fargo Rewards offers a better 1:2 transfer ratio.)
Many people associate Choice with their budget or midscale brands, so it can be considered an under-the-radar program. However, Choice has several upscale brands including Cambria Hotels, the Ascend Hotel Collection and Radisson Blu. These are the types of properties where you can often find great value for your points.
Another excellent way to use your Choice points is through its partnership with Preferred Hotels & Resorts, a collection of independent properties. For example, you can book a hotel room in Tokyo for 35,000 Choice points (or 23,334 Citi points) a night. That’s significantly less than what you’d often find with other major hotel loyalty programs.
Citi airline transfer partners: What you need to know
You can get incredible value by transferring your Citi points to American’s AAdvantage program, and it isn’t that hard to do so. Compared to United MileagePlus and Delta SkyMiles, American’s loyalty program consistently has excellent availability at reasonable redemption rates.
As a founding member of the Oneworld alliance, American has access to not just seats on American but also its partners, unlocking a plethora of opportunities to travel the world for budget and bougie travelers alike.
Aeromexico Rewards is a SkyTeam airline, meaning you can use its points to book flights on Aeromexico and any of its alliance partners. Still, it can be complicated to navigate Aeromexico’s platform, and for many, it’s not worth the hassle.
As a Star Alliance member, Avianca can be a good way to book flights on partners like United Airlines, Lufthansa and Turkish Airlines.
However, the program has gone through multiple devaluations, eliminating many of its long-time sweet spots. But there are still deals to be found — assuming you have the patience to navigate its clunky (and sometimes rage-inducing) online booking platform.
Another Oneworld member, Cathay Pacific allows you to book flights on Cathay Pacific and its partner airlines. If you’re looking to splurge, the Hong Kong-based carrier offers both business and first class on certain flights, which can be a great way to travel to Asia. For the best business class experience, try Cathay Pacific’s newest Aria Suite.
Citi points transfer to Emirates Skywards at a ratio of 1:0.8 or 1:0.56 — the only airline partner that doesn’t transfer at a 1:1 or 1:0.7 ratio. That instantly makes it harder to get the best value when transferring to Emirates. But if you’re looking to fly in Emirates’ glitzy business- or first-class cabins, booking with Skywards is often the way to go. Just be aware that you’ll need to pay high taxes and fees for that privilege — and you’ll need to have elite status just to book Emirates first class awards.
Like Emirates, Etihad Airways is based in the United Arab Emirates. It also offers excellent premium cabin experiences, as long as you’re willing to shell out for them. There aren’t a ton of sweet spots to be had here, but if you’re looking to fly in The Residence — Etihad’s three-room suite for up to two guests available on its Airbus A380s — you’ll need to use Etihad Guest miles to upgrade your first-class ticket purchase.
This Taiwanese Star Alliance carrier offers a luxurious way to travel between the U.S. and Asia. While you can book partner awards via Infinity MileageLands, you’re better off using your miles to book the airline’s Royal Laurel business class since EVA tends to offer better award availability to its own loyalty members.
Flying Blue is the loyalty program shared by SkyTeam airlines Air France and KLM. That means you can use your Flying Blue miles to book not just Air France and KLM flights, but also those on partners like Delta Air Lines and Virgin Atlantic. Some ways to boost the value of your Flying Blue miles — which NerdWallet estimates at 0.8 cent each — include booking premium cabins, monthly Promo Rewards that offer discounted awards on select routes, and free stopovers in Amsterdam and Paris.
JetBlue TrueBlue is Citi’s other U.S.-based airline transfer partner. While the airline is not part of a major alliance, it does have numerous partnerships with airlines like Condor and Qatar Airways. It’s also linked up with United via the airlines’ Blue Sky partnership , which allows for award redemption on United using TrueBlue points. NerdWallet values JetBlue points at 1.4 cents apiece.
Qantas Frequent Flyer is the loyalty program for Qantas, Australia’s flag carrier. The airline is also a member of Oneworld, which means its points theoretically can be valuable. However, the program underwent a devaluation in August 2025, gutting a program that didn’t have great redemption options to begin with.
Members of Qatar Airways Privilege Club earn Avios, a points currency shared by other airlines like British Airways, Aer Lingus, Iberia and Finnair. A unique feature of the program is the ability to transfer Avios between loyalty programs that use the currency, meaning you can search for different sweet spots in each program. While Qatar is a Oneworld member, the best way to use your Qatar Airways Avios is booking the airline’s popular Qsuites business class product.
Singapore Airlines is a Star Alliance member, but it also partners with outside airlines like Alaska Airlines. That means you can use your KrisFlyer miles on a variety of redemptions, ranging from Singapore’s business and first class experiences to economy flights on Alaska for a Hawaiian beach vacation. If you’re not interested in going to Singapore, the airline also offers routes between Los Angeles and Tokyo, as well as New York and Frankfurt.
Thai Airways is yet another Star Alliance member, and it’s one of Citi’s most unique airline transfer partners. That’s because Royal Orchid Plus is not a well-known program among U.S. travelers, and no other major transferable currency partners with the program. But if you’re willing to take the time to learn the program’s quirks, you can get good value — especially if you’re interested in flying Thai Airways’ business or first class cabins.
Turkish Airlines Miles&Smiles rounds out Citi’s roster of Star Alliance transfer partners. Like Avianca LifeMiles, this program has gone through a devaluation and introduced pricing tactics that make it less attractive than before. Still, there are still some sweet spots, such as promotional business class awards on Turkish Airlines to Istanbul or one-way domestic flights on United for just 10,000 miles.
Virgin Atlantic Flying Club is the loyalty program for London-based Virgin Atlantic, a SkyTeam member. The program uses dynamic pricing, which means you can often find extremely cheap award redemptions, but you can also find outrageously expensive ones as well. Keep in mind that booking through Virgin Atlantic Flying Club often comes with high taxes and fees. If you’re looking for some Virgin Atlantic sweet spots, check out premium cabin redemptions on non-alliance partner airlines like All Nippon Airways.
Citi hotel transfer partners: What you need to know
Accor Live Limitless transfers from Citi at a 2:1 or 2:0.7 ratio, meaning you have to spend at least 2 Citi points to get 1 ALL point. According to ALL, its points have a fixed value, meaning it's hard to get outsized value from them.
Choice Privileges is an often overlooked program that can deliver compelling value. With a transfer ratio of 1:1.5 or 1:1.05, it’s worth checking out, especially if you’re traveling to expensive cities or making last-minute bookings. (If you have Wells Fargo Rewards, though, keep in mind those points transfer to Choice at a better 1:2 ratio.) You might get even more value by leveraging the Choice Privileges partnership with Preferred Hotels & Resorts , where each property offers Choice members fixed redemption rates as long as there is availability.
Leaders Club is the loyalty program for The Leading Hotels of the World, a collection of luxury properties around the world. The transfer ratio can be discouraging (5:1 or 5:0.7), but that’s because Leaders Club points are worth approximately 8 cents each. With a 5:1 transfer ratio, that gives your Citi points a value of about 1.6 cents each when transferred to Leaders Club. Keep in mind that many of these hotels can be extremely pricey (we’re talking well over $1,000 a night), but it can certainly be worth it if you’re big on luxury.
If Preferred Hotels & Resorts sounds familiar, that’s because we just talked about it with Choice Privileges. However, Citi also transfers directly to I Prefer Hotel Rewards, the loyalty program for the independent collection. It offers a superior 1:2 or 1:1.4 ratio, although that doesn’t necessarily mean these points are worth more than Choice points. To get the best deal, you’ll want to look at both programs before booking.
Wyndham Rewards is the last of Citi’s hotel transfer partners, and this one can also offer excellent value for guests. It uses a fixed redemption chart, where each room costs either 7,500, 15,000 or 30,000 points a night. This is another great option when you’re traveling to popular tourist destinations or during peak travel periods.
You can earn Citi ThankYou points via spending on eligible Citi credit cards. One of the easiest ways to earn a large stash of these points is by signing up for a new card and meeting the minimum spending requirement to earn a welcome bonus.
Cards that earn Citi ThankYou points typically have “Citi ThankYou” or “Citi Strata” in their names, but there are also some cash-back cards that earn cash back in the form of ThankYou points. Remember that Citi ThankYou cards with annual fees generally have access to more favorable transfer ratios than those without.
🤓 Nerdy Tip
If you have multiple Citi cards that earn ThankYou points, you can combine them to take advantage of a card that offers higher transfer ratios.

last-minute flight, you can almost always count on american to have availability at reasonable award prices. (nerdwallet values american miles at 1.3 cents each when booking economy flights.) american miles are also an excellent choice for redeeming bucket-list flights, such as japan airlines first class or finnair’s innovative “no recline” business class seat. those premium redemptions are pricey, so they have largely been out of reach for many until july 2025, when citi added american as a transfer partner — the only major transferable currency that currently does so. hotel transfer partners are usually lackluster, because their points aren’t always worth that much. there are a few exceptions to that rule, and choice privileges is one of them. citi is not the only major transferable program that transfers to choice, but it does offer a higher 1:1.5 transfer ratio — meaning you get 1.5 choice points for every citi point you transfer. (however, wells fargo rewards offers a better 1:2 transfer ratio.) many people associate choice with their budget or midscale brands, so it can be considered an under-the-radar program. however, choice has several upscale brands including cambria hotels, the ascend hotel collection and radisson blu. these are the types of properties where you can often find great value for your points. another excellent way to use your choice points is through its partnership with preferred hotels & resorts, a collection of independent properties. for example, you can book a hotel room in tokyo for 35,000 choice points (or 23,334 citi points) a night. that’s significantly less than what you’d often find with other major hotel loyalty programs. citi airline transfer partners: what you need to know you can get incredible value by transferring your citi points to american’s aadvantage program, and it isn’t that hard to do so. compared to united mileageplus and delta skymiles, american’s loyalty program consistently has excellent availability at reasonable redemption rates. as a founding member of the oneworld alliance, american has access to not just seats on american but also its partners, unlocking a plethora of opportunities to travel the world for budget and bougie travelers alike. aeromexico rewards is a skyteam airline, meaning you can use its points to book flights on aeromexico and any of its alliance partners. still, it can be complicated to navigate aeromexico’s platform, and for many, it’s not worth the hassle. as a star alliance member, avianca can be a good way to book flights on partners like united airlines, lufthansa and turkish airlines. however, the program has gone through multiple devaluations, eliminating many of its long-time sweet spots. but there are still deals to be found — assuming you have the patience to navigate its clunky (and sometimes rage-inducing) online booking platform. another oneworld member, cathay pacific allows you to book flights on cathay pacific and its partner airlines. if you’re looking to splurge, the hong kong-based carrier offers both business and first class on certain flights, which can be a great way to travel to asia. for the best business class experience, try cathay pacific’s newest aria suite. citi points transfer to emirates skywards at a ratio of 1:0.8 or 1:0.56 — the only airline partner that doesn’t transfer at a 1:1 or 1:0.7 ratio. that instantly makes it harder to get the best value when transferring to emirates. but if you’re looking to fly in emirates’ glitzy business- or first-class cabins, booking with skywards is often the way to go. just be aware that you’ll need to pay high taxes and fees for that privilege — and you’ll need to have elite status just to book emirates first class awards. like emirates, etihad airways is based in the united arab emirates. it also offers excellent premium cabin experiences, as long as you’re willing to shell out for them. there aren’t a ton of sweet spots to be had here, but if you’re looking to fly in the residence — etihad’s three-room suite for up to two guests available on its airbus a380s — you’ll need to use etihad guest miles to upgrade your first-class ticket purchase. this taiwanese star alliance carrier offers a luxurious way to travel between the u.s. and asia. while you can book partner awards via infinity mileagelands, you’re better off using your miles to book the airline’s royal laurel business class since eva tends to offer better award availability to its own loyalty members. flying blue is the loyalty program shared by skyteam airlines air france and klm. that means you can use your flying blue miles to book not just air france and klm flights, but also those on partners like delta air lines and virgin atlantic. some ways to boost the value of your flying blue miles — which nerdwallet estimates at 0.8 cent each — include booking premium cabins, monthly promo rewards that offer discounted awards on select routes, and free stopovers in amsterdam and paris. jetblue trueblue is citi’s other u.s.-based airline transfer partner. while the airline is not part of a major alliance, it does have numerous partnerships with airlines like condor and qatar airways. it’s also linked up with united via the airlines’ blue sky partnership , which allows for award redemption on united using trueblue points. nerdwallet values jetblue points at 1.4 cents apiece. qantas frequent flyer is the loyalty program for qantas, australia’s flag carrier. the airline is also a member of oneworld, which means its points theoretically can be valuable. however, the program underwent a devaluation in august 2025, gutting a program that didn’t have great redemption options to begin with. members of qatar airways privilege club earn avios, a points currency shared by other airlines like british airways, aer lingus, iberia and finnair. a unique feature of the program is the ability to transfer avios between loyalty programs that use the currency, meaning you can search for different sweet spots in each program. while qatar is a oneworld member, the best way to use your qatar airways avios is booking the airline’s popular qsuites business class product. singapore airlines is a star alliance member, but it also partners with outside airlines like alaska airlines. that means you can use your krisflyer miles on a variety of redemptions, ranging from singapore’s business and first class experiences to economy flights on alaska for a hawaiian beach vacation. if you’re not interested in going to singapore, the airline also offers routes between los angeles and tokyo, as well as new york and frankfurt. thai airways is yet another star alliance member, and it’s one of citi’s most unique airline transfer partners. that’s because royal orchid plus is not a well-known program among u.s. travelers, and no other major transferable currency partners with the program. but if you’re willing to take the time to learn the program’s quirks, you can get good value — especially if you’re interested in flying thai airways’ business or first class cabins. turkish airlines miles&smiles rounds out citi’s roster of star alliance transfer partners. like avianca lifemiles, this program has gone through a devaluation and introduced pricing tactics that make it less attractive than before. still, there are still some sweet spots, such as promotional business class awards on turkish airlines to istanbul or one-way domestic flights on united for just 10,000 miles. virgin atlantic flying club is the loyalty program for london-based virgin atlantic, a skyteam member. the program uses dynamic pricing, which means you can often find extremely cheap award redemptions, but you can also find outrageously expensive ones as well. keep in mind that booking through virgin atlantic flying club often comes with high taxes and fees. if you’re looking for some virgin atlantic sweet spots, check out premium cabin redemptions on non-alliance partner airlines like all nippon airways. citi hotel transfer partners: what you need to know accor live limitless transfers from citi at a 2:1 or 2:0.7 ratio, meaning you have to spend at least 2 citi points to get 1 all point. according to all, its points have a fixed value, meaning it's hard to get outsized value from them. choice privileges is an often overlooked program that can deliver compelling value. with a transfer ratio of 1:1.5 or 1:1.05, it’s worth checking out, especially if you’re traveling to expensive cities or making last-minute bookings. (if you have wells fargo rewards, though, keep in mind those points transfer to choice at a better 1:2 ratio.) you might get even more value by leveraging the choice privileges partnership with preferred hotels & resorts , where each property offers choice members fixed redemption rates as long as there is availability. leaders club is the loyalty program for the leading hotels of the world, a collection of luxury properties around the world. the transfer ratio can be discouraging (5:1 or 5:0.7), but that’s because leaders club points are worth approximately 8 cents each. with a 5:1 transfer ratio, that gives your citi points a value of about 1.6 cents each when transferred to leaders club. keep in mind that many of these hotels can be extremely pricey (we’re talking well over $1,000 a night), but it can certainly be worth it if you’re big on luxury. if preferred hotels & resorts sounds familiar, that’s because we just talked about it with choice privileges. however, citi also transfers directly to i prefer hotel rewards, the loyalty program for the independent collection. it offers a superior 1:2 or 1:1.4 ratio, although that doesn’t necessarily mean these points are worth more than choice points. to get the best deal, you’ll want to look at both programs before booking. wyndham rewards is the last of citi’s hotel transfer partners, and this one can also offer excellent value for guests. it uses a fixed redemption chart, where each room costs either 7,500, 15,000 or 30,000 points a night. this is another great option when you’re traveling to popular tourist destinations or during peak travel periods. you can earn citi thankyou points via spending on eligible citi credit cards. one of the easiest ways to earn a large stash of these points is by signing up for a new card and meeting the minimum spending requirement to earn a welcome bonus. cards that earn citi thankyou points typically have “citi thankyou” or “citi strata” in their names, but there are also some cash-back cards that earn cash back in the form of thankyou points. remember that citi thankyou cards with annual fees generally have access to more favorable transfer ratios than those A Complete Guide to American Airlines Award Travel Rules
by Tyler Glatt Carissa Rawson
June 15, 2025
Reviewed by JT Genter
Add us as a trusted source on Google
Takes just a few seconds
AwardWallet receives compensation from advertising partners for links on the blog.
The opinions expressed here are our own and have not been reviewed, provided, or approved by any bank advertiser.
Here's our complete list of Advertisers .
American Airlines AAdvantage ® is one of our favorite mileage programs. Thanks to dozens of partners , you can fly to over 1,000 destinations using AAdvantage miles. And American Airlines has (largely) kept its partner mileage redemption chart the same for nearly a decade now. In this post, we'll cover all the rules for American Airlines award travel to help you use miles for your next adventure.
One of the key ways to get more value from your rewards is to spend a little time learning the rules for booking awards with the points and miles you acquire. Each program has its own set of strengths and weaknesses. Once you understand them clearly, you’ll be able to choose the program that best suits your goals for a particular trip.
Page Contents
Types of Rules
American Airlines AAdvantage Award Travel Costs & Partner Airlines
Award pricing
Redemption partners
American Airlines Award Booking Rules and Fees
Which airlines can I book using American AAdvantage miles?
Can I use my miles to book for another person?
How far in advance can I book my ticket?
Can I put an AA award travel reservation on hold?
Does American charge a close-in booking fee?
Does American charge a phone booking fee?
Can I upgrade my flight with AAdvantage miles?
How much does it cost to include a lap infant?
American Airlines Award Ticket Cancellation Policy
How much does American Airlines charge to change or cancel an award ticket?
Does American Airlines allow free changes within 24 hours?
How can I track award flight changes?
American Airlines Award Travel Itinerary Rules
Can I book a stopover with American miles?
Can I book one-way travel with American miles?
Can I book an open jaw with American miles?
Can I fly with multiple partner airlines on the same trip?
Can I book an itinerary with multiple classes of service?
Can I book a round-the-world ticket with American miles?
American third-region exceptions
Itinerary Rules for Complex Award Bookings
Published route requirement
Maximum Permitted Mileage (MPM)
American Airlines award segment limits
Married segment logic
Final Thoughts
Booking rules cover the details you need to know to get your trip confirmed. Can you use your miles to book a flight for your grandma? How much does it cost to add an infant who doesn’t need her own seat? How far in advance can you confirm your flights?
Change and cancellation rules cover all the fine print to ensure you don’t pay unnecessary fees and know exactly what to expect if your plans change.
Itinerary rules cover your options and limitations for the flights you add to your trip. For example, can you add a stopover in Europe on the way to Asia? Can you book a one-way flight at half the price of a round-trip? Will you save miles if part of a business-class trip is in economy?
Credit: Joshua Hanson/Unsplash
Before we get started, two important factors aren’t covered in this post: award pricing and redemption partners.
The number of miles you need and the taxes and fees you’ll pay out of pocket are determined by the type of points you redeem. In many cases, the costs in miles and money can be more important than other rules. This post isn't about how much you'll spend for an AA award or a comparison of AA's booking prices against competitors. Note that AAdvantage miles are separate from Loyalty Points . The former is what you can use to book award flights, while the latter is used to determine the level of elite status you have with American Airlines .
Related : AAdvantage Platinum status: upgrades, lounge access, free checked bags, and more benefits
You can use AAdvantage miles to book travel with more than 20 partner airlines . But it’s also true that you can use dozens of different types of points to book flights operated by American or its partner airlines. If you aren’t familiar with this, check out our beginner’s guide to award travel planning .
If you’ve done a good job of diversifying your points and miles, you should have the option to use rewards from other frequent-flyer programs to book a similar (or identical) itinerary. Here are the rules for redeeming AAdvantage miles for their best uses .

While AAdvantage doesn’t impose fuel surcharges on most partners, it passes along significant fees on some airlines — such as British Airways — especially in premium cabins. Be sure to review the total taxes and fees before booking.
Yes, American Airlines will let you book a flight for anyone you wish. However, program terms and conditions prohibit selling miles, so you shouldn’t book for people you don’t know or receive payment to book on someone else's behalf.
When paying for the taxes and fees on award tickets, American Airlines requires that the name on the credit card match the name of the AAdvantage account holder. Thus, your brother can't use your credit card when booking a ticket using his miles.
Related: Rules on Booking Award Flights for Other People
American Airlines opens award bookings around 331 days before departure. However, it's worth noting that many partners — like Qatar Airways, British Airways, and Japan Airlines — release award space earlier through their own programs.
Related: How Far in Advance Can You Book Airline Award Tickets?
Yes, on eligible reservations you can put a hold for up to 24 hours. These must be booked at least seven days in advance.
American Airlines no longer charges a close-in-booking fee. In February 2020, American Airlines eliminated its $75 close-in booking fee .
What is this? Some frequent flyer programs charge an extra fee when you book a ticket within 21 days of departure. American Airlines award travel no longer has this fee.
Credit: Hassan Ouajibir/Unsplash
American Airlines no longer charges a fee to book reservations over the phone. Before November 2020 , AA charged a $25 fee to book travel within the U.S. by phone but waived this fee for tickets that couldn't be booked on AA.com. The fee jumped to $35 for international travel, but this is gone also.
Yes, you can upgrade eligible tickets using your AAdvantage miles . However, this option isn't available on economy awards or basic economy tickets.
The cost to add a lap infant to an AAdvantage award depends on where you're flying:
International awards: American charges 10% of the cash price of an adult ticket to add a lap infant to your reservation. If you’re flying on a business- or first-class award, 10% of the retail ticket cost can be a lot of money.
Domestic awards: Within the U.S. or to Canada, Puerto Rico, or the U.S. Virgin Islands, there is no additional cost.
You can add your child to your ticket by calling AA reservations at 800-433-7300. If travel is within the U.S. (including Puerto Rico), you also can add a lap infant online when booking a trip or modifying an existing reservation.
Pro tip: If you're flying internationally, it's often best to book your award ticket first and then call American to add your lap infant. That's because AA will charge the full mileage price if you add an infant at the time of booking. So, this extra step can help you avoid being charged additional AAdvantage miles — though, as is often the case, your experience may depend on the agent.
Here’s American Airlines' resource on traveling with children and infants.
Related: These are the best award programs for traveling with a lap infant
The AAdvantage program really shines with its rules for travel changes and cancellation policy. The airline eliminated change and cancel fees in November 2020 and (re)added the ability to change certain awards in July 2023 .
American Airlines no longer charges a fee to change or cancel an award ticket. Before November 11, 2020, American Airlines charged up to $150 per passenger to change or cancel an award ticket — unless you had Executive Platinum elite status.
American Airlines first class on a Boeing 787 aircraft. Credit: J.T. Genter/AwardWallet
Now, you can cancel award tickets and have your miles reinstated free of charge up to one year after the ticket was issued. The latest you can cancel an award ticket is before the first flight in your itinerary departs.
The U.S. Department of Transportation requires airlines serving the U.S. to allow travelers to put itineraries on hold or to change or cancel a ticket for 24 hours after booking without a penalty.
Related: 24-Hour Cancellation Policies for Major U.S. Airlines
AwardWallet tracks all your travel plans automatically (and alerts you to any changes) by importing reservations from your email or connected accounts. The best method for getting all your travel plans into AwardWallet is to link the email you use for reservations.
American Airlines does not allow free stopovers on award travel. Any connection of 24 hours or more on an international award will result in paying for more than one award ticket.
American Airlines and Alaska Airlines have a lot of redemption partners in common. Alaska MileagePlan allows free stopovers as long as you follow a few rules. That combines with the fact there are some awards we recommend booking with Alaska miles instead of American miles .
Yes, you can book one-way awards with American Airlines AAdvantage miles. AAdvantage generally charges half the price of a round-trip award for one-way itineraries — but this is only guaranteed on partner awards. On some AA-operated awards, AAdvantage offers a cheaper per-price flight on round-trip bookings than the cost of two one-way awards.
Pro tip: For international tickets, be aware that many countries require proof of onward travel. Thanks to American's one-day hold policy, you can create a record locator for a return trip without actually confirming the ticket. If you prefer to have a confirmed reservation, consider booking a fully refundable ticket or using a ticket-rental service like OneWayFly .
American Airlines first class seat on Boeing 777-300ER. Credit: J.T. Genter/AwardWallet
American allows open-jaw tickets at both the origin and destination. An open jaw ticket that includes more than one region will be priced as two one-way awards.
If you don't know what we're talking about, check out our beginner’s guide to stopovers and open jaws .
Yes, American allows you to combine partners on an award ticket. Award prices are the same for all partner airlines, so there shouldn't be any extra cost if your flights are operated by more than one carrier.
American prices its awards according to the highest class of service you book on your itinerary. This differs from some programs like Avianca LifeMiles , which charge a prorated number of miles based on the percentage of your trip in each class. With American, you won't save any miles by booking one flight in economy and other flights in business class.
However, American does not have any rules that prohibit you from booking a ticket with different classes of service (you may see this referred to as a “mixed-cabin award”). This can be a good thing. In some situations, you might not be able to find business- or first-class awards for the entire trip.
For example, if you're flying from Amsterdam (AMS) to Boston (BOS) via New York (JFK), you might find a business-class ticket across the Atlantic, but the short flight from New York to Boston might only be available in economy. You can lock in that award, and if the short flight becomes available in business class, you might be able to upgrade without paying any extra miles.
Main Cabin on an American Airlines Boeing 787. Credit: American Airlines
Most domestic “first-class” awards are treated as business class when you pair them with an international flight. In other words, you won't be charged the first-class price on an international business-class award when you add a domestic flight in AA's domestic first class.
Pro tip: American's mixed-cabin award rules generally work in your favor. But be aware that American will happily charge you the business-class price for itineraries with a 10-hour flight in economy and a 45-minute flight in business class.
American uses a zone-based AAdvantage award chart for partners which means that the price of travel between two regions (like Europe and the Continental U.S.) should be consistent, regardless of the specific origin and destination airports. Prague to Los Angeles should cost the same number of miles as Dublin to Boston — even though the former is twice the distance! This isn't the case for flights operated by AA, which prices its awards dynamically. And trips with stops in more than one award region (as defined here ) are not a strength of the AAdvantage program.
Related : My trick to booking an American Airlines award that includes a partner
When your itinerary connects at an airport that is not in the same region as your origin or destination , American Airlines may charge you for each trip separately. For example, if you book a flight directly from Paris to New York, you’ll pay the Europe-to-U.S. price on the award chart. But if you fly from Paris to Doha (in the Middle East region) and then to New York, you’ve transited a “third region”.
As a general rule on award travel booked with American Airlines, you’ll pay more when you transit through a third region. But American has a list of exceptions that don’t require separate awards.

Unless otherwise noted, North America includes the regions of the contiguous U.S., Alaska, Hawaii, Canada, Mexico, and the Caribbean.
You can find an in-depth discussion of the third region exceptions for AA award travel in this FlyerTalk forum . However, note that some of the content is outdated.
The remaining routing rules aren't likely to be a factor for most award bookings. However, if you find yourself unable to book the flights you want online, one of these could be the problem. It's worth noting that American's reservations system is known to make mistakes. If you see a price in miles that is higher than what you expect based on the award chart, check these rules first.
It's also worth noting that few American reservations agents understand these rules (or even know they exist). If the computer says 110,000 miles, that's what the agent will try to charge you. Fortunately, the AAdvantage program does have a process for manually ticketing reservations when the system makes a mistake. If you've checked these advanced rules and the price doesn't look right, ask your agent to check with the rates desk or a supervisor.
American Airlines' general award travel rule is that awards must be booked on a published route. More specifically, the airline that flies you across the ocean (the overwater carrier) needs to sell a ticket between your origin and destination.
Let's take a trip to the Maldives, for example. There's no better way to go than in Etihad's Apartments. However, in order to book an AAdvantage award, Etihad has to sell a ticket between your origin and Male (MLE). To confirm this, you'll want to check ExpertFlyer's Fare Information search . If you're unfamiliar with the tool, check out our guide to ExpertFlyer before proceeding further.
While it's not as exact, you also can search the route on Google Flights and see if there's an option to book on Etihad.
Credit: Google
If you're departing from a major hub, such as New York City or Los Angeles, you aren't going to have any issues finding a ticket sold by Etihad:
Credit: ExpertFlyer
However, there are no published fares on Etihad between Billings, Montana (BIL) and Male:
Thus, you wouldn't be able to book an AAdvantage award on Etihad flights on this route. If you run into this dilemma, it may still be possible to book the ticket depending on the agent who's ticketing the award. Otherwise, you might need to buy a separate “positioning” flight from the smaller airport to a major hub with a published fare to your final destination.
Related: Free Holds, Maximizing Award Regions, Other Tricks for Booking Awards Using AAdvantage Miles
“Maximum Permitted Mileage” is a lovely piece of jargon that means exactly what it says. For each origin and destination, there is a maximum number of miles you can travel. This American Airlines award travel rule relies primarily on the “published route” requirement explained above. However, in cases where your desired award ticket isn't a published route, you may still be able to book it if the itinerary is less than 125% of the MPM.
Unfortunately, the MPM isn't published online. ExpertFlyer members can check the MPM under the “Travel Information” section:
American allows up to two connections (three flight segments) on one-way domestic awards. You can add one more connection (four segments) on one-way international awards.
For round-trip travel, you must follow these same limits in each direction of travel. Your destination is the first stop that lasts more than 24 hours (international) or 4 hours (domestic). If your trip requires more than this limit, American Airlines will charge you for more than one award.
In the past, American Airlines released award availability flight by flight. Say you wanted to fly from Los Angeles (LAX) to Frankfurt (FRA). If you found award availability on a flight from Los Angeles to Dallas–Fort Worth and on a flight from Dallas–Fort Worth to Frankfurt, you could book the award. Likewise, someone just flying from Dallas to Frankfurt also could book an award on just that nonstop flight.
That all changed in late 2017. Instead of releasing award availability by flight, American Airlines now releases award availability by trip . Under these rules, you might only be allowed to book the Dallas to Frankfurt flight if you start in Los Angeles or some other city — but not in Dallas. If you want to start in Dallas, you might need book a connecting flight through Charlotte or Miami on the way to Frankfurt to book the cheapest award. Married segment logic might prohibit booking a nonstop flight from A to B on certain routes.
This irritating way of releasing award availability is referred to as “married segment logic.” That's because the award availability for each individual flight is “married” to the entire itinerary. Put simply, American uses these rules to make the cheapest awards less convenient with the hope of getting you to pay more miles for a nonstop award.
American Airlines AAdvantage miles can be extremely valuable if you know how and when to use them and are aware of the basic rules for award travel. While there is certainly a learning curve for more complex itineraries, there are plenty of awards you can book at a great price with limited experience.
American's policy for putting award tickets on hold is among the most generous of any frequent flyer program. When planning travel that will require more than one type of miles, it's a huge asset to be able to lock in your AAdvantage award for up to five days while you figure out the other parts of your trip.
The rules for changing tickets are another standout feature of this program. American Airlines eliminated award mileage redeposit fees, so you no longer have to pay a fee to cancel an award.
The AAdvantage booking rules are more complex than revenue-based programs like Southwest Rapid Rewards and JetBlue TrueBlue . However, a little extra effort to learn how this program works can pay huge dividends.
Tip of The Day
Did you know you can see the transaction history for most of your loyalty program accounts in AwardWallet? In your AwardWallet account dashboard, click the + sign next to the account you want to view. Then, click the History tab to see your individual transaction history.

than one award. in the past, american airlines released award availability flight by flight. say you wanted to fly from los angeles (lax) to frankfurt (fra). if you found award availability on a flight from los angeles to dallas–fort worth and on a flight from dallas–fort worth to frankfurt, you could book the award. likewise, someone just flying from dallas to frankfurt also could book an award on just that nonstop flight. that all changed in late 2017. instead of releasing award availability by flight, american airlines now releases award availability by trip . under these rules, you might only be allowed to book the dallas to frankfurt flight if you start in los angeles or some other city — but not in dallas. if you want to start in dallas, you might need book a connecting flight through charlotte or miami on the way to frankfurt to book the cheapest award. married segment logic might prohibit booking a nonstop flight from a to b on certain routes. this irritating way of releasing award availability is referred to as “married segment logic.” that's because the award availability for each individual flight is “married” to the entire itinerary. put simply, american uses these rules to make the cheapest awards less convenient with the hope of getting you to pay more miles for a nonstop award. american airlines aadvantage miles can be extremely valuable if you know how and when to use them and are aware of the basic rules for award travel. while there is certainly a learning curve for more complex itineraries, there are plenty of awards you can book at a great price with limited experience. american's policy for putting award tickets on hold is among the most generous of any frequent flyer program. when planning travel that will require more than one type of miles, it's a huge asset to be able to lock in your aadvantage award for up to five days while you figure out the other parts of your trip. the rules for changing tickets are another standout feature of this program. american airlines eliminated award mileage redeposit fees, so you no longer have to pay a fee to cancel an award. the aadvantage booking rules are more complex than revenue-based programs like southwest rapid rewards and jetblue trueblue . however, a little extra effort to learn how this program works can pay huge dividends. tip of the day did you know you can see the transaction history for most of your loyalty program accounts in awardwallet? in your awardwallet account dashboard, click the + sign next to the account you want to view. then, click the history tab to see your individual transaction history. share this post load comments travel better with effortless itinerary organization connect your email or forward your travel plans. see your reservations in United MileagePlus Program: The Complete Guide
The U.S.-based airline's MileagePlus loyalty program lets passengers easily earn and redeem miles.
Written by
Benjamin Din
Lead Travel Writer & Content Strategist
11 years of experience

loyalty programs
Benjamin Din is a lead travel writer at NerdWallet. He previously was a technology reporter at Politico, where he authored a daily newsletter covering tech and telecom policy.
Benjamin loves to travel — both for work and for fun. He’s reported from three continents and visited more than 45 countries. He has written for The Wall Street Journal, Bloomberg News, The Washington Post, The San Francisco Chronicle and The (Johannesburg) Star, as well as covered two Olympics with NBC Sports.
His goal is to visit a new country and a new state each year.

United MileagePlus is the frequent flyer program for United Airlines, which is among the largest airlines in the U.S. and the world.
The airline operates 4,500 daily flights to more than 300 cities across five continents. As a founding member of Star Alliance , United has partnerships with dozens of airlines, including some outside of the alliance. These relationships mean you can earn and redeem United miles on more than 40 airlines while traveling to almost anywhere in the world.
To get the most out of the United MileagePlus program, you’ll likely want a United credit card , which can unlock perks like free checked bags, lounge access and higher mileage earning rates when you fly with the airline.

To earn miles with United Airlines, you’ll first need to sign up for a MileagePlus frequent flyer account . It’s free to join. With a MileagePlus account, you'll also get access to free Starlink Wi-Fi on United , when available.
And if you’re an infrequent United flyer, relax: Your United miles will not expire , as long as your account remains open.
United Economy Plus seats. (Photo courtesy of United Airlines)
Based on our most recent analysis, NerdWallet values United MileagePlus miles at 1.2 cents apiece. To determine the value of reward miles, we compared cash prices and reward redemptions for economy round-trip routes across several destinations and dates.
We divided the cost of the cash ticket by the cost of the reward ticket to determine a “cent per mile” value for each flight, then averaged this value across several flights and dates. Read more about how we arrived at these figures .
NerdWallet’s valuation is a baseline value, based on real-world data collected from hundreds of economy routes, not a maximized value. In other words, aim for award redemptions that offer at least 1.2 cents or more in value from your United miles.
Use our United miles calculator to determine the value of your miles.
Back to top

By signing up, you will receive newsletters and promotional
content and agree to our Terms of Use and acknowledge the data practices in our Privacy Policy . You may unsubscribe at any time.
United Airlines came in fifth place among overall airlines in NerdWallet’s most recent analysis of the best airlines .
Its rewards program, MileagePlus, came in fourth place in an analysis of the best airline loyalty programs .
How to earn United miles
You can earn miles when you fly on paid tickets with United or its partner airlines, by using a United Airlines credit card, and by shopping and dining with partners.
MileagePlus miles can be redeemed for award flights and many other uses, though flight redemptions typically offer the most value. United miles do not expire, as long as your account remains open and in good standing.
United’s economy cabin. (Photo courtesy of United Airlines)
The total miles you'll earn for a United flight depends on how much you spent on the ticket, your status level within the MileagePlus program, the fare you purchased and whether you hold a United credit card.
When you purchase a United flight, United MileagePlus members without elite status will earn 0 miles on basic economy fares. Main cabin and premium fares will earn a base 3 miles per $1 spent on airfare and airline fees.
NOTE: You earn miles only on airfare and airline fees. Government-imposed taxes and fees do not earn miles.
That base number can increase (or decrease) depending on several factors:
1. Whether you hold a United credit card
If you hold a United credit card, you will earn at least 6 miles per $1, regardless of whether you pay with the card. That means eligible cardholders earn at least double the number of United miles compared to non-cardholders.
However, if you have the United Gateway Card or the United MileagePlus Debit Rewards card, you’ll need to spend $10,000 in a calendar year to unlock the higher cardmember earn rate. (More on the different credit and debit card options below.)

If you have children under 18, you can share your cardmember earn rate with them by linking your MileagePlus accounts. Authorized users and other people traveling on the same reservation will not earn the cardholder earn rate unless they are the primary cardholder of their own United cards.
2. Your Premier elite status level
United’s Premier elite status members earn additional miles based on their status level. The higher your status, the more miles you earn.
3. Your purchased fare
If you purchase a basic economy fare, you will earn 3 fewer miles per $1. That means a MileagePlus general member without a United credit card will earn 0 miles on basic economy fares.
However, if you have elite status, a United credit card or both, you will still earn miles. To calculate how many miles you will earn on a basic economy fare, use the table above and subtract three to get your earn rate.
Earning on other airlines: When the ticket is issued by United (ticket number starting with 016), you’ll earn miles based on the cost of the fare. You can earn up to 75,000 miles per ticket. But if your ticket is booked through one of United’s partners, miles are awarded based on a calculation involving the fare class of the ticket and the flight distance. One exception is flights operated by JetBlue, which will earn based on how much you spent, the fare type and United elite status, thanks to the airlines' Blue Sky partnership .
United Airlines offers co-branded credit cards through Chase. Bonuses between cards vary, but you can generally expect cards with the highest annual fees to have the best welcome offers.
For casual United flyers, NerdWallet's favorite United credit card is the United℠ Explorer Card . It earns 3 miles per dollar spent on United purchases, 2 miles per dollar at restaurants and on hotel stays, and 1 mile per dollar on all other purchases.
